serving.description: 1 whole fish (700–800g live weight, approximately 400–500g edible flesh after cooking)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of soon hock is one whole steamed fish weighing approximately 700–800g live weight, yielding around 400–500g of edible flesh, typically shared among 3–4 diners at a Chinese seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Oxyeleotris marmorata, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Fish Composition; adapted using steamed freshwater fish reference values from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA Foundation Foods (Striped bass, steamed, as closest analogous freshwater/estuarine fish)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for marble goby (Oxyeleotris marmorata). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed freshwater fish entries (striped bass steamed, NDB 15149; snakehead fish from FAO/INFOODS SEA tables) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed fish entries. Marble goby is a lean, white-fleshed freshwater fish with nutritional profile closely comparable to steamed snakehead or sea bass. Macros reflect steamed whole fish edible portion per 100g. Soy sauce and sesame oil condiments contribute minor sodium; values reflect typical restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish and Seafood — University of Sydney GI Database; plain steamed fish has negligible glycemic impact due to near-zero carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Marble goby is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Steamed fish as a food category has a GI of effectively 0 due to negligible carbohydrate content (0.5g per 100g). GI is set to 0 with gi_category 'low' per convention for protein-dominant, near-zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per 450g serving = (0 x 0.5 x 4.5) / 100 = 0.0. This is consistent with peer-reviewed literature confirming that plain fish has no measurable glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and cooking condiments
hypertension.guidance: Soon hock steamed with soy sauce contributes approximately 180mg sodium per 100g edible portion; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion to 150–200g edible flesh per meal to keep sodium intake within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high-quality lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed marble goby contains only 0.5g carbohydrate per 100g with a glycemic index of effectively zero, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; it supports glycaemic control and satiety without raising postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Marble goby is a lean white fish with only 0.5g saturated fat per 100g and a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; regular consumption of steamed fish is consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns, though sodium from soy sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soon Hock (笋壳鱼), known in English as Marble Goby (Oxyeleotris marmorata), is a highly prized freshwater fish in Singapore and Southeast Asian Chinese cuisine. It is celebrated for its exceptionally sweet, delicate, and firm white flesh with minimal bones, making it one of the most sought-after and expensive live fish served at Chinese seafood restaurants. The fish is almost always prepared by steaming whole — a method that preserves its natural sweetness and tender texture — and is typically finished with a drizzle of hot oil, light soy sauce, ginger strips, and spring onions. The dish described here is a whole fish of 700–800g live weight, yielding approximately 400–500g of edible flesh. Nutritionally, steamed marble goby is an excellent source of high-quality lean protein (approximately 18.5g per 100g), very low in fat (2.1g per 100g), and contains negligible carbohydrates, giving it a glycemic index of effectively zero. It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. The primary nutritional concern is sodium contributed by soy sauce in the preparation. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and weight, though sodium-conscious individuals should request reduced soy sauce. In Singapore, soon hock is a prestige dish often ordered for family celebrations, business dinners, and festive occasions, and commands a premium price reflecting its rarity and flavour profile.
common_user_queries: Is soon hock good for diabetics?
What does 笋壳鱼 taste like?
Soon hock vs sea bass nutrition
Is soon hock high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central entry exists specifically for marble goby (Oxyeleotris marmorata). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest analogous steamed freshwater fish entries (snakehead, striped bass, sea bass) from USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA tables, cross-referenced with HPB steamed fish data. The adaptation is defensible given the similar lean white-flesh profile of these species, but exact values for marble goby may differ slightly. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from analogous steamed freshwater fish sources (USDA SR Legacy steamed fish entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA) as no direct database entry for Oxyeleotris marmorata exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. 2. Sodium value (180mg/100g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with light soy sauce; plain steamed fish without soy sauce would have approximately 60–80mg sodium per 100g. 3. glycemic_index is set to 0 because steamed fish contains negligible carbohydrates (0.5g per 100g) and has no measurable glycemic response; gi_category is 'low' per convention for near-zero-carbohydrate protein foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate contribution is negligible. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain steamed fish per USDA SR Legacy data. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from analogous freshwater fish entries; these are -1 sentinel values replaced with best-estimate numeric values from analogous sources. 6. The input cuisine_tag 'Crab' appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; this record is for marble goby fish, not crab. 7. Price SGD 63.22 reflects premium live fish pricing at The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon), The Riverwalk, Singapore.
